description: Vaccine/autism belief-survey abstracts. Correct label is 'neither' — they report what people BELIEVE about a causal claim, not biomedical evidence for it. skimgpt 2.0.1 systematically mislabeled these as 'supports_H2' (see SKiM_web km_query 26 row 318, yr 2020 iter 1: 13/20 abstracts labeled supports_H2, mostly from this failure mode).
hypothesis_1: Autism is caused by Genetic predisposition.

items.title: Beliefs in vaccine as causes of autism among SPARK cohort caregivers
items.abstract_excerpt: 16.5% of caregivers endorsed immunizations as perceived causes of autism. Children of these caregivers had more regression and worse outcomes, reflecting belief in vaccine causation.
items.why: Survey of caregiver BELIEFS about causation. Measures prevalence and correlates of a belief, not whether the belief is true.

items.title: Vaccines and autism: a preliminary qualitative study on the beliefs of concerned mothers in Italy
items.abstract_excerpt: Interviewed mothers; the main theme was childhood vaccines as a perceived cause of ASD. About one third reported ASD as a combination including childhood vaccines.
items.why: Qualitative interview study of maternal beliefs.

items.title: Who or What Influences the Individuals' Decision-Making Process Regarding Vaccinations?
items.abstract_excerpt: Support for vaccination was lower among respondents believing in a vaccine-autism relationship. The survey directly measures belief that vaccination and autism are associated.
items.why: Survey using 'believing in' / 'belief that' as its outcome variable.

items.title: Factors associated with childhood vaccine hesitancy and measles vaccine hesitancy among healthcare students
items.abstract_excerpt: Many healthcare students believed vaccines are related to autism. The study reports vaccine-autism beliefs among respondents.
items.why: Beliefs of healthcare students about a causal claim.

items.title: Vaccine Hesitancy and Attributions for Autism among Racially and Ethnically Diverse Groups of Parents
items.abstract_excerpt: All vaccine-hesitant parents agreed that toxins in vaccines were a cause of their child's ASD. The study directly examines attributions of ASD to vaccines.
items.why: Study of parental ATTRIBUTIONS — a belief construct, not biomedical evidence.

items.title: The impact of parents' health behaviours on their preferences regarding vaccinations in Bialystok, Poland
items.abstract_excerpt: Participants who reported that vaccines can cause autism engaged in higher preventive behaviors. The abstract explicitly reports belief that vaccines can cause autism.
items.why: Correlates a belief with other behaviors. Belief, not evidence.

items.title: Beliefs about causes of autism and vaccine hesitancy among parents of children with autism spectrum disorder
items.abstract_excerpt: Parents who were vaccine hesitant more often endorsed vaccines as a cause of ASD. The paper analyzes beliefs linking vaccines to autism among parents of children with ASD.
items.why: Title literally begins 'Beliefs about causes' — paper analyzes a belief, not biomedical evidence.

items.title: Online Information of Vaccines: Information Quality, Not Only Privacy, Is an Ethical Responsibility of Search Engines
items.abstract_excerpt: Search engines often return anti-vaccine pages for 'vaccines autism' queries. Study addresses misinformation around vaccines and autism.
items.why: Study of search-engine misinformation. Documents that the topic is widely discussed online; does not test the causal claim.
